evidence.source: Gartner press release, June 25 2025: 'Gartner Predicts Over 40% of Agentic AI Projects Will Be Canceled by End of 2027'

evidence.claim: S&P Global Market Intelligence's 2025 Voice of the Enterprise survey (1,000+ IT and business leaders, North America and Europe) found that the share of companies abandoning most of their AI initiatives rose from 17% in 2024 to 42% in 2025, with the average organization scrapping 46% of proofs-of-concept before reaching production. Separately, 46% of respondents at organizations that had invested in generative AI reported that no single enterprise objective had seen a 'strong positive impact' from that investment.
evidence.source: S&P Global Market Intelligence, Voice of the Enterprise: AI & Machine Learning 2025; summarized at spglobal.com/market-intelligence

evidence.claim: A March 2026 survey of 650 enterprise technology leaders found 78% have active AI agent pilots but only 14% have reached production scale -- a 64-point gap. Among organizations that attempted to expand pilots, 64% hit blocking issues, and 72% of those have been stalled for six months or more. The five root causes accounting for 89% of scaling failures are: integration complexity with legacy systems (cited by 63%), inconsistent output quality at volume (58%), absent monitoring and observability tooling (54%), unclear organizational ownership (49%), and insufficient domain training data (41%).
evidence.source: Digital Applied, 'AI Agent Scaling Gap March 2026: Pilot to Production', proprietary survey of 650 technology leaders, February-March 2026

camps.label: Narrow agents ship; general agents don't
camps.proponents: Gartner analysts (August 2025 task-specific agent forecast); Salesforce (Agentforce internal deployment data, FY25-FY27); Andrej Karpathy (LLM-as-kernel architecture critique); Digital Applied enterprise survey researchers (650-leader study, Feb-Mar 2026); domain-specific agent vendors including UiPath, IBM watsonx Orchestrate, and Glean
camps.steelman:
The claim is not that agentic AI is failing -- it is that the only agentic AI actually shipping into production is scoped, task-defined, and measurably bounded. Broad, open-ended agents remain stuck in pilot purgatory for structural reasons that current LLM capability cannot overcome.

The core logic runs as follows. A production system must satisfy four simultaneous constraints: reliable output quality at volume, auditability and governance, integration stability with existing enterprise systems, and predictable cost. Narrow agents can satisfy all four because their task boundary eliminates most of the compound-error surface. An agent that classifies documents, routes support tickets, or extracts fields from loan applications operates over a well-characterized input distribution, produces outputs that can be automatically evaluated, and fails in predictable, containable ways. Every successful production deployment on record -- Salesforce Agentforce handling case resolution, SDR agents doing outbound qualification, UiPath agents executing structured RPA workflows -- is narrow by design.

General-purpose agents fail all four constraints simultaneously. Multi-function orchestrators experience output quality degradation at volume because small per-step errors compound across action sequences. They produce outputs that cannot be automatically evaluated without another model, creating circular governance dependencies. They require integration with dozens of legacy APIs, each a new failure mode. And their cost-per-task scales unpredictably with task complexity.

The pilot-to-production gap is not an engineering execution problem that investment will close -- it reflects an architectural truth: the problem space of a general agent is combinatorially larger than any test suite can cover, so every production incident is a novel edge case. Narrow agents shrink that surface to something testable. Until reasoning models develop reliable self-verification and chain-of-thought auditing matures to production standards, "agentic AI in production" is a category that empirically contains only narrow agents. Vendor narratives about general autonomy describe roadmap, not deployed reality.

evidence.claim: Gartner forecasts 40% of enterprise applications will feature task-specific AI agents by end-2026, up from under 5% in 2025 -- the framing is explicitly task-specific, not general-purpose. The prediction implicitly acknowledges that what is crossing into production is narrow by definition.
evidence.source: Gartner press release, August 26 2025: 'Gartner Predicts 40% of Enterprise Apps Will Feature Task-Specific AI Agents by 2026'

evidence.claim: A February-March 2026 survey of 650 enterprise technology leaders found 78% have active AI agent pilots but only 14% have reached production scale. Among those that failed to scale, successful deployments were exclusively narrow: 'agents scoped to a single, well-defined task with measurable outputs -- a document classifier, a data enrichment pipeline, a routing agent. Agents designed to handle broad, open-ended tasks failed at scale.' Broad multi-function agents experienced 'compounding quality variance and untestable edge cases.'
evidence.source: Digital Applied, 'AI Agent Scaling Gap March 2026: Pilot to Production' (proprietary 650-leader survey)

evidence.claim: Salesforce's own Agentforce deployment -- cited as the most documented large-scale agentic production case -- handled case resolution and lead outreach at narrow, pre-defined tasks: 84% of support cases resolved without human intervention in Q4 FY25, and 130,000 leads contacted over four months. Accuracy drops sharply as scope widens: simple narrow agents achieve 95% accuracy; complex multi-source agents fall to 80-90%; general DIY agents plateau at 50-60% -- operationally unacceptable for autonomous production use.
evidence.source: Cyntexa, 'Agentforce Statistics and Trends (2025-2026)', citing Salesforce FY25-FY27 internal metrics

camps.label: The governance bar for "production" isn't met
camps.proponents: Forrester analysts Brian Hopkins, Craig Le Clair, Jeff Pollard, Rowan Curran, and Leslie Joseph; Gartner research division; FINRA regulatory staff (2026 oversight report); EU AI Act compliance analysts at appliedAI and Cloud Security Alliance; Microsoft Research AI safety researchers (Wenyue Hua et al.); academic AI reliability researchers (arXiv 2602.16666 Princeton-affiliated study of 14 frontier models)
camps.steelman:
Enterprise software has always had a production bar that goes beyond "it works in demos." That bar includes: deterministic auditability (you can reconstruct what happened and why), regulatory traceability (a human can be held accountable for every consequential action), failure containment (a bad output fails safely rather than cascading), and operational SLAs (the system behaves consistently at volume under adversarial conditions). Agentic AI systems, by their nature, violate every one of these properties by design.

The core problem is not capability -- it is controllability. An agent that can invoke tools, spawn subagents, write to databases, and execute multi-step plans across systems is operating in a fundamentally different risk class than a chatbot or a classifier. When such a system hallucinates, it does not produce a wrong sentence -- it executes a wrong action. That action may be irreversible. No mainstream agentic framework today provides the combination of immutable audit logs, deterministic replay, scoped permissions, and policy-enforced escalation that would let a regulated enterprise sign off on autonomous operation. These are not features vendors are working on; they are properties that require rethinking the architecture from the ground up.

The deployment data confirms this. Vendors and the press talk about "going production," but the numbers tell a different story: roughly 79% of enterprises have agents somewhere in their environment, but only 11% run them in anything that could honestly be called production -- meaning unsupervised, at scale, on consequential workflows. That 68-point gap is not explained by slow adoption cycles. It is explained by the fact that legal, risk, and compliance teams are looking at these systems and refusing to sign off. Gartner's own June 2025 forecast -- that over 40% of agentic AI projects will be canceled by end of 2027 -- cites rising costs, unclear ROI, and insufficient risk controls as the reasons. The technology is not the bottleneck; governance infrastructure is.

The regulatory environment is tightening around exactly this gap. FINRA issued its first-ever formal warning about AI agent hallucinations in its 2026 oversight report, requiring broker-dealers to build explicit procedures for agents acting "beyond the user's actual or intended scope and authority." The EU AI Act's August 2026 high-risk compliance deadline requires conformity assessments, human oversight mechanisms, logging, and technical documentation that most enterprises cannot yet produce -- an appliedAI analysis of 106 deployed enterprise AI systems found that 40% could not even be clearly classified under the Act's risk tiers, let alone made compliant. These are not future requirements; they are current law.

The "production" announcements coming from vendors are, almost uniformly, about narrow, low-stakes, human-supervised tasks: summarizing documents, drafting responses, triaging tickets. That is not what "agentic" means in the technical literature or in the vendor pitch decks. The genuine agentic vision -- autonomous multi-step execution across enterprise systems with real consequences -- is nowhere near the governance maturity required to deploy it at scale in a regulated industry. Calling a glorified chatbot with a tool call "agentic AI in production" is vendor narrative. The production bar, properly understood, has not been met.

evidence.claim: Gartner forecast (June 2025): over 40% of agentic AI projects will be canceled by end of 2027 due to insufficient risk controls, unclear ROI, and governance gaps -- not because the underlying technology failed.
evidence.source: Gartner press release, June 25, 2025

evidence.claim: Forrester senior analysts (Hopkins, Le Clair, et al.) document that while 75% of enterprise leaders claim agentic AI adoption, only a small minority have it running in meaningful production beyond 'agentish' chatbots; true scaled multi-agent systems are rarer still, and more than half of enterprises report governance issues even after adopting the NIST AI RMF.
evidence.source: Forrester blog: The State of Agentic AI in 2026 -- Companies Are Chasing, Few Are Catching

evidence.claim: FINRA's 2026 regulatory oversight report issued its first-ever formal warning specifically about AI agent hallucinations in financial services, requiring broker-dealers to develop procedures for agents acting beyond the user's actual or intended scope -- a direct acknowledgment that no adequate production governance framework yet exists for agentic systems in regulated industries.
evidence.source: Fortune, April 8, 2026: 'What do you do when your AI agent hallucinates with your money?'

inference-time-scaling-reasoning-models.name: Inference-Time Scaling and Reasoning Models
inference-time-scaling-reasoning-models.whatItIs: Reasoning models are AI systems that work through a problem step by step before answering, instead of replying with the first thing that comes to mind. Inference-time scaling is the idea that you can make these models smarter simply by letting them spend more time and computing power thinking at the moment you ask a question, rather than only making the underlying model bigger during its initial build. In short: a smaller model that is allowed to think longer can often beat a much larger model that answers instantly.
inference-time-scaling-reasoning-models.whatsHappening: By mid-2026, paying a model to think longer at the moment you ask it a question, rather than only building a bigger model, has become a central story of the AI industry. At its GTC conference in March 2026, NVIDIA reframed data centers as token factories and argued that reasoning models, which spend extra compute thinking before they answer, now drive compute demand far more than training larger base models, citing growth in some inference workloads of up to about 10,000 times over two years. OpenAI turned the idea into a literal product control on March 5, 2026 with GPT-5.4 Thinking and Pro, which expose five dialable reasoning-effort levels so users and developers can spend more or less thinking compute per request and trade cost against quality. The research is catching up to the slogans: a wave of 2026 scaling-law papers, including Test-Time Scaling Makes Overtraining Compute-Optimal, jointly optimizes model size, training data, and thinking budget so labs can decide when paying for longer thinking beats paying for a bigger model. Analysts at Deloitte and McKinsey describe a broad shift of AI compute from training toward inference, the practical sign that this technique now shapes hardware, products, and budgets across the field. The honest caveat is cost: thinking longer is not free, and on easy questions the extra compute buys little, so the open question is how to spend a thinking budget only where it actually helps.
inference-time-scaling-reasoning-models.leans: Complementary and conditional: a knob subordinate to base-model quality, valuable only where it pays
inference-time-scaling-reasoning-models.rationale: Three of the four proposed camps collapse. Product Pragmatists ("dial thinking budget to problem difficulty") and Efficiency Skeptics ("jointly optimize size/data/thinking, longer thinking buys little on easy questions") assert the same substance — spend inference compute only where it pays — differing only in venue (product knob vs. research optimization). Compute-Shift Optimists is orthogonal: NVIDIA/consultancy "token factory" framing is a business observation about where compute money flows right now, which is compatible with every stance on the actual axis and so isn't a genuine opponent. What survives is one real tension with two poles: is inference-time scaling a durable PRIMARY architectural bet, or a complementary, task-conditional lever whose ceiling is still set by base-model quality (the Training-Primacy view)? That is a tradeoff, not 3-4 distinct camps. Evidence leans toward the complementary/conditional pole: the survey itself notes the industry has accepted the technique and disagrees only on degree, durability, and spend allocation; actual products expose it as a dialable knob (implying it's not always worth it); even the optimists frame it as allocation shift, not replacement of training. The pure "primary path that subsumes base-model scaling" position has few committed defenders. Not forced to 50/50 — the weight clearly sits on complementary-but-real.

camps.label: Durable primary path: inference-time compute is a structural, lasting lever
camps.proponents: Noam Brown (OpenAI researcher, creator of Pluribus and Cicero, lead voice on test-time scaling); Google DeepMind (Gemini Deep Think, AlphaProof teams); the ICLR 2025 "Inference Scaling Laws" research community; Sebastian Raschka (ML researcher and author tracking the empirical record); analysts and researchers documenting the training-to-inference compute inversion (e.g., Introl Research, AI Barcelona)
camps.steelman:
Inference-time compute is not a patch on weak base models -- it is a structurally distinct, orthogonal axis of scaling that unlocks capabilities no amount of additional pre-training can replicate at equivalent cost. The core insight is that intelligence on hard problems is not fixed at training time; it is a function of how much search, verification, and deliberation a system performs at the moment of answering. This mirrors how humans solve novel hard problems: not by recalling a memorized answer but by thinking through it step by step, backtracking, and checking work.

Three structural reasons this is durable, not temporary:

First, the pre-training axis is hitting a genuine ceiling. Internet-scale text is finite and largely consumed. Returns to adding more tokens or parameters to base models are diminishing sharply, and the costs to push the frontier through training alone have become economically prohibitive before reaching the capability levels the field targets. Inference scaling opens a second axis that does not share this bottleneck: compute at inference time can be increased arbitrarily on demand, targeted per query, and cheapened continuously as hardware improves.

Second, the efficiency ratio strongly favors inference compute in the domains that matter most. Noam Brown has argued publicly that 1 unit of test-time compute is equivalent to 1,000 to 10,000 times more in model size for reasoning tasks. Empirically: a 1B-parameter model with heavy inference scaling can outperform a 405B Llama 3 model on structured reasoning benchmarks; OpenAI o3 at high compute scored 88% on ARC-AGI-1 (surpassing the human baseline for the first time) using a configuration that would have required an implausibly larger base model to match through training alone. In verifiable domains -- mathematics, formal proof, competitive programming -- inference scaling compounds with reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) to produce capabilities that have no plausible training-only path: Gemini Deep Think solved five of six IMO 2025 problems for a gold-medal score operating end-to-end in natural language within the 4.5-hour competition window, using parallel exploration of multiple solution paths at inference time.

Third, inference scaling is theoretically principled, not ad hoc. The July 2025 "Directed Stochastic Skill Search" framework (arxiv 2507.00004) provides a unified analytical model showing that chain-of-thought, tree-of-thought, best-of-N, and majority voting are all instances of the same underlying principle: stochastic traversal of a learned skill graph. This framework recovers the empirically observed "linear accuracy gain with logarithmic compute" scaling law and, critically, shows that reasoning capabilities emerge during inference even when performance has plateaued under parameter scaling. This is not a collection of tricks; it is a second scaling law with its own closed-form relationships. The fact that every major frontier lab -- OpenAI (o1/o3/o4), Google (Gemini Deep Think), Anthropic (extended thinking), DeepSeek (R1) -- has independently converged on this paradigm is strong evidence of structural durability, not fashion.

The holders of this view do not claim inference scaling replaces training. They claim it is a permanent, co-equal dimension: base models set a capability floor, and inference compute determines how far above that floor a system can reach on any given problem. As hardware costs fall and inference efficiency improves (Brown's "underappreciated point": models are becoming more efficient in how they think, achieving more with the same test-time budget), the practical cost objections weaken over time. The arc of the argument is that by the time smarter base models arrive, inference scaling will be cheaper and more capable still -- making it a permanent structural feature of frontier AI, not a bridge to something else.

evidence.claim: OpenAI o3 at high compute scored 87.5% on ARC-AGI-1, surpassing the human baseline for the first time and using approximately 172x more compute per problem than standard inference. This is direct empirical proof that inference-time compute unlocks qualitatively new capability ceilings unreachable by the base model alone.
evidence.source: ARC Prize / OpenAI o3 ARC-AGI-Pub Breakthrough, December 2024

evidence.claim: Google DeepMind's Gemini Deep Think achieved gold-medal standard at the 2025 International Mathematical Olympiad (5/6 problems, 35/42 points), operating entirely in natural language within the competition time limit. The system used parallel inference-time exploration of multiple solution paths -- a capability enabled by extended thinking compute, not by model size alone.
evidence.source: Google DeepMind blog: Advanced version of Gemini with Deep Think officially achieves gold-medal standard at the IMO

evidence.claim: The July 2025 'Directed Stochastic Skill Search' paper provides a unified theoretical framework showing that inference-time scaling follows its own principled scaling law (linear accuracy gains with logarithmic compute) and that reasoning capabilities emerge during inference even when parameter-scaling has plateaued -- establishing inference scaling as a foundational, not incidental, dimension of LLM optimization.
evidence.source: arxiv 2507.00004: A Theory of Inference Compute Scaling: Reasoning through Directed Stochastic Skill Search

camps.label: Complementary and conditional: inference scaling is a knob subordinate to base-model quality, valuable only where it pays
camps.proponents: ML researchers and practitioners who accept test-time compute as a useful tool but contest its framing as a primary or autonomous scaling axis: the authors of "The Limits of Inference Scaling Through Resampling" (arXiv 2411.17501, 2024-25); Zhao, Hooi, and Ng, authors of "Test-Time Scaling in Reasoning Models Is Not Effective for Knowledge-Intensive Tasks Yet" (arXiv 2509.06861, 2025); the ICLR 2025 paper on scaling LLM test-time compute optimally vs. model parameters (which empirically establishes domain-conditioned substitutability); Yann LeCun (Meta/AMI) who argues LLMs lack grounded world knowledge that inference alone cannot supply; Francois Chollet (ARC-AGI designer) whose ARC-AGI-2 benchmark was explicitly designed with compute bounds to prevent brute-force inference scaling from masking generalization failures; and the broader ML efficiency research community studying compute-optimal inference tradeoffs (Wu et al., arXiv 2408.00724, ICLR 2025)
camps.steelman:
Inference-time scaling is a genuine, usable knob -- but it is a second-order amplifier, not a first-order source of capability. Its payoff is strictly bounded by what the base model already knows and can already verify.

The strongest version of this position runs as follows:

1. The information-theoretic hard ceiling. Test-time compute is post-processing of a fixed trained model. By the data-processing inequality, no amount of additional sampling, chain-of-thought, or tree search can introduce factual knowledge the base weights do not contain. On knowledge-intensive tasks, extended reasoning does not recover missing facts -- it amplifies hallucination, because the model generates supporting detail to fill gaps it cannot actually fill (Zhao et al., 2509.06861). The ceiling on what inference scaling can recover is set entirely by pretraining.

2. The verifier dependency problem. The most principled forms of inference scaling (best-of-N, MCTS, resampling-based data curation) require a reliable verifier or process reward model. Weaker base models have higher false-positive rates -- they produce wrong solutions that pass verifiers -- and this rate cannot be reduced by sampling more; it accumulates. There is therefore a mathematical upper bound on accuracy achievable through resampling that is itself a function of base model quality. A stronger base model lowers false-positive rates and thus raises the ceiling that inference scaling can reach (arXiv 2411.17501). Inference scaling and base model quality are multiplicative, not additive.

3. Domain conditionality is sharp, not gradual. The ICLR 2025 compute-optimal inference paper establishes that on easy-to-medium problems -- those within the base model's existing capability frontier -- test-time compute substitutes efficiently for additional pretraining. On hard problems that lie outside the base model's capability, pretraining is strictly preferable. This is not a smooth tradeoff; it is a phase transition. Inference scaling is cheap and effective within the base model's competence envelope, and increasingly wasteful outside it. The practical implication: inference scaling cannot expand what a model can do; it can only improve reliability within what the model could already do, given enough tries.

4. Distillation forecloses the "just think longer" argument over time. When a large reasoning model's extended chain-of-thought outputs are distilled into a smaller model via supervised fine-tuning, the smaller model recovers most of the performance gain without paying the per-query inference cost. DeepSeek-R1 distillation demonstrated that a 7B model trained on reasoning traces from a large teacher outperforms non-reasoning models at GPT-4o scale. This means the gains attributed to inference scaling are partially artifacts of insufficient base training: a better-trained base model simply bakes in what the larger model had to think through at runtime. Inference scaling is therefore a temporary inefficiency that better pretraining and distillation pipeline iteratively absorbs.

5. The cost curve is nonlinear in the wrong direction. Inference scaling produces sublinear quality gains against superlinear compute increases. On ARC-AGI-1, OpenAI's o3 required $20,000 per task (high-compute mode) versus $20 per task (low-compute mode) for an improvement from roughly 76% to 88% -- a 12-point gain for a 1,000x cost increase. ARC-AGI-2 collapsed this to near zero performance at any compute level for the same model, demonstrating that the gains were within-distribution to the training domain rather than evidence of general reasoning ability. A model that genuinely understood the underlying structure would not require five orders of magnitude more compute to squeeze out incremental gains.

The steelman conclusion: inference scaling is a valuable and legitimate engineering lever -- particularly for verifiable domains like math and code, at moderate budget multiples, and for well-trained base models. But it is not a durable primary path to capability improvement because its ceiling is set by the base model, its cost structure is unfavorable at the frontier, and its gains are progressively internalized by better pretraining and distillation. The correct mental model is not "inference scaling vs. pretraining" but "inference scaling as a loan against future pretraining improvements" -- useful when held short-term, increasingly expensive if carried indefinitely.

evidence.claim: Test-time scaling does not consistently improve accuracy on knowledge-intensive tasks and frequently increases hallucinations, because additional compute cannot create factual knowledge beyond what is already encoded in the trained model -- an information-theoretic constraint. On SimpleQA, GPT-4o-mini's hallucination ratio increased by over 15% as reasoning tokens grew from 300 to 3,300.
evidence.source: Zhao, Hooi, and Ng. 'Test-Time Scaling in Reasoning Models Is Not Effective for Knowledge-Intensive Tasks Yet.' arXiv 2509.06861, September 2025.

evidence.claim: Resampling-based inference scaling has a hard mathematical ceiling set by the base model's false-positive rate: if a stronger model's single-sample accuracy exceeds a weaker model's accuracy even conditioned on passing the verifier, no compute budget can close that gap. False positives accumulate with more samples, making the optimal number of resampling attempts often 5 or fewer, regardless of available compute.
evidence.source: 'The Limits of Inference Scaling Through Resampling.' arXiv 2411.17501v3, 2024-25.

evidence.claim: Test-time compute and pretraining are not freely interchangeable. On easy-to-medium problems within the base model's capability envelope, inference scaling substitutes efficiently for pretraining. On hard problems that lie outside the base model's capability, pretraining is strictly more effective -- establishing that inference scaling is conditional on base model reach, not a universal substitute for it.
evidence.source: Scaling LLM Test-Time Compute Optimally Can Be More Effective Than Scaling Model Parameters. ICLR 2025 (proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/1b623663fd9b874366f3ce019fdfdd44).

small-language-models-on-device-ai.name: Small Language Models and On-Device AI
small-language-models-on-device-ai.whatItIs: A small language model, or SLM, is a compact version of the same kind of AI that powers chatbots, shrunk down to roughly 1 to 13 billion adjustable settings instead of the hundreds of billions in the biggest cloud models. Because they are small, these models can run directly on your phone or laptop using its own chip, rather than sending your words to a company's servers. "On-device AI" is the umbrella term for this: the AI lives on the hardware in your hand, so it works without internet, keeps your data private, and responds almost instantly.
small-language-models-on-device-ai.whatsHappening: Mid-2026 is the moment on-device AI went mainstream across all three big platforms within a single month. At Build 2026 (June 2), Microsoft unveiled Aion 1.0, a new family of on-device models: Aion 1.0 Instruct for everyday text tasks (open-sourcing on Hugging Face in July) and Aion 1.0 Plan, a 14-billion-parameter reasoning and tool-calling model with a 32K context window that ships in-box for fully local agents. Then on June 11, Microsoft extended Windows 11's local language-model APIs beyond premium Copilot+ machines to any PC with an NVIDIA RTX 30-series or newer GPU and just 6GB of video memory, effectively opening local AI to tens of millions of existing computers. Apple followed at WWDC on June 8 with its third-generation Foundation Models: a refreshed 3-billion-parameter on-device model plus AFM 3 Core Advanced, a 20-billion-parameter sparse model that cleverly activates only 1 to 4 billion parameters per request so it fits on a phone. NVIDIA's open Nemotron 3 Nano (30B total, 3B active) targets on-device agents, Qualcomm now runs 8-billion-parameter models on Snapdragon 8 Elite phones, and Dell branded 2026 "the year of the SLM," citing Gartner's forecast that organizations will use small task-specific models three times more than general LLMs by 2027.
small-language-models-on-device-ai.leans: Cloud retains the high-value work and absorbs the rest
small-language-models-on-device-ai.rationale:
Pressed adversarially, the four camps collapse toward one fundamental tension, not four distinct stances. Camp 3 (Privacy/Sovereignty) and Camp 4 (Capability Gap) are not rival predictions about the same axis — they are inputs to it. Privacy/sovereignty is a normative constraint that simply widens the set of tasks forced onto the edge (it strengthens the edge pole); the capability gap is the engineering reality that caps how much can move there (it strengthens the cloud pole). Neither makes an independent claim about whether edge or cloud captures the majority of inference compute, which the survey itself names as "the real disagreement." Fold them in and you are left with exactly two poles of a single spectrum: how much durable inference share migrates to the device. That is a tradeoff, not a multi-camp debate. The survey's own notes concede "most technical actors agree on the near-term hybrid picture" — everyone accepts hybrid; they only disagree on the eventual mix. That is the signature of one tension with two ends, so "binary"/"multiple" would manufacture separation that does not exist among the actors.

Why not skip: there is a real, unresolved disagreement about the long-run equilibrium (does edge become the default substrate or stay a preprocessing layer?), so presenting only one pole would be a genuine omission, not consensus. The contestedness (0.65) and the live commercial stakes justify showing the tension.

Leans toward the cloud-absorption pole on current weight of evidence: hyperscalers and frontier labs ship the leading SLMs themselves (Apple AFM, Microsoft Phi, Google Gemma), own the developer tooling and distribution, and reasoning-heavy/long-context work — the high-margin tier — still demonstrably exceeds what 3B–14B on-device models do reliably. The edge wins specific, high-volume, latency- or privacy-bound tasks and that share is real and growing, but those are largely the lower-value tasks, and the same incumbents capture them too. The structural displacement claim (serious workloads permanently leaving the cloud and out of incumbent hands) remains the weaker-supported side today. Not 50/50, but not a blowout — the privacy/sovereignty constraint is a genuine, non-commoditizable carve-out (regulated data that legally cannot leave the device) that keeps the edge pole live. No fresh research was done, so this reflects the well-documented public positions in the survey rather than newly verified data.

camps.label: Edge captures durable share (latency, privacy, cost)
camps.proponents: Apple (on-device-first intelligence architecture with Private Cloud Compute as fallback), Microsoft (Copilot+ PC / Phi Silica mandatory OS deployment), Qualcomm (Snapdragon X NPU platform), Meta (Llama 3.2 1B/3B edge-optimized variants), Google (Gemma 3n mobile-native models), Gartner (analyst forecast on SLM volume dominance by 2027), and enterprise infrastructure analysts at MindStudio and ComputeForecast who model inference unit economics
camps.steelman:
The cloud-first inference model has a structural ceiling defined by three compounding constraints: latency physics, regulatory jurisdiction, and marginal cost economics. On-device SLMs resolve all three simultaneously, which is why the shift is architectural rather than merely tactical.

On latency: network round-trips to cloud inference endpoints add 200-500ms of irreducible overhead. On-device inference on 2025 mobile silicon runs at 32ms for production-class models. That gap is not closable by cloud providers no matter how fast their GPUs get -- physics of packet transit do not compress. For voice assistants, AR overlays, real-time translation, and autonomous systems, the latency floor of cloud inference makes it architecturally disqualified, not merely inconvenient.

On privacy and regulatory pressure: GDPR enforcement has accelerated sharply (EU fines reached 2.3 billion euros in 2025 alone, up 38% year-over-year). HIPAA, DORA, and the EU AI Act (fully applicable August 2026) are driving healthcare, finance, and public sector workloads toward local processing as a compliance necessity, not a preference. Apple's architecture makes this explicit: its ~3B-parameter on-device model handles the default case, and only anonymized sub-tasks route to Private Cloud Compute. This is not a privacy marketing position -- it is a hard engineering constraint that cloud providers cannot match for data that must never leave the device.

On economics: once a model is downloaded, each additional inference has zero marginal cost. Cloud API billing at scale (500M tokens/day generates approximately 15,000 dollars/month at mid-tier pricing) becomes untenable for high-frequency, low-complexity tasks. A 7B SLM costs 10-30x less to serve than a 70B+ frontier model, and at device scale the comparison is not 10-30x cheaper -- it is zero ongoing cost versus a compounding API bill. This is why Gartner projects organizations will deploy task-specific SLMs at 3x the volume of general-purpose LLMs by 2027, and why Gartner also forecasts 143 million AI PC shipments in 2026, representing 54.7% of the worldwide PC market.

The strongest version of this argument is not that SLMs replace cloud AI for frontier reasoning tasks -- they do not. The argument is that the vast majority of AI inference workload by volume consists of narrow, repeated, latency-sensitive tasks (classification, summarization, transcription, suggestion, translation) where a 3-7B model achieves 70-95% of GPT-class performance at a fraction of the cost. Cloud providers will retain dominance for complex reasoning and training, but the high-volume inference tail -- which is where the monetization actually lives at consumer and enterprise scale -- durably migrates to the edge. Hardware vendors (Apple, Qualcomm, Intel, AMD, Microsoft) are embedding NPUs in every new device class, making edge inference a default capability rather than a specialized deployment. That ubiquity, combined with the structural impossibility of eliminating network latency and the accelerating regulatory cost of cloud data processing, means edge captures durable share even as cloud inference also grows.

evidence.claim: Gartner projects that by 2027 organizations will deploy small, task-specific AI models at 3x the volume of general-purpose LLMs, and forecasts 143 million AI PC shipments in 2026 (54.7% of worldwide PC market), with 40% of software vendors prioritizing on-device AI capabilities by end of 2026, up from 2% in 2024.
evidence.source: Byteiota analysis of Gartner forecasts on SLM adoption and AI PC shipments

evidence.claim: Apple's production on-device-first architecture routes the default inference workload through a ~3B-parameter model on Apple Silicon, escalating only anonymized sub-tasks to Private Cloud Compute -- with the explicit security rationale that data existing only on user devices is disaggregated and not subject to any centralized point of attack.
evidence.source: Apple Security Research Blog, Private Cloud Compute architecture overview

evidence.claim: EU GDPR enforcement fines reached 2.3 billion euros in 2025 alone (up 38% year-over-year, totaling 5.65 billion euros since 2018), and the EU AI Act becomes fully applicable in August 2026, driving healthcare, finance, and public sector organizations to repatriate AI workloads to local or sovereign infrastructure as a compliance requirement.
evidence.source: Kiteworks, Data Privacy in 2026: What the Regulatory Surge Means for Your Data

camps.label: Cloud Retains the High-Value Work and Absorbs the Rest
camps.proponents: Microsoft (Satya Nadella, Azure AI), Google DeepMind (Demis Hassabis), Andreessen Horowitz cloud-skeptic analysts, enterprise SaaS vendors running orchestration layers on cloud APIs, NVIDIA (Jensen Huang on inference-at-scale), Gartner enterprise AI analysts
camps.steelman: On-device SLMs are real and useful, but they are a triage layer, not a replacement layer. The work that matters economically -- complex multi-step reasoning, long-context analysis, cross-domain synthesis, agentic orchestration, and novel problem-solving -- still requires frontier-scale models that will never fit on consumer hardware at competitive quality. Cloud providers are not being displaced by on-device AI; they are absorbing it. Microsoft ships Phi for the edge while collecting $10B+ annually from Azure OpenAI inference. Google ships Gemini Nano while selling the frontier Gemini model to Apple for roughly $1B/year as the backbone of Apple Intelligence. The pattern is consistent: the cloud hyperscalers own both the on-device model distillation pipeline and the frontier model that on-device systems escalate to when they fail. SLMs structurally cannot close the gap on multi-step reasoning, broad world knowledge, or extended context -- benchmarks confirm accuracy degrades sharply for out-of-distribution and cross-domain tasks as parameter count drops. Meanwhile the economic flywheel is spinning faster on the cloud side, not slower: agentic AI deployments, which Gartner projects will be embedded in 40% of enterprise applications by end-of-2026, require frontier orchestrator models at the top of every agent hierarchy. Edge inference handles the cheap, repetitive, latency-sensitive tail; cloud captures every high-stakes decision, every complex workflow, and every training run. Far from being disrupted, the hyperscalers have made on-device AI their distribution channel -- they publish the SLMs, they sell the APIs the SLMs call when they hit their limits, and they charge for the cloud compute that trains the next generation of both. The architectural shift is real, but the value does not migrate with it.

evidence.claim: Microsoft's AI inference revenue hit a $10B annual run rate by Q1 2025, generated entirely by inference workloads (not training), with Satya Nadella stating the company was turning away training requests because inference demand was so high -- demonstrating that cloud inference is accelerating, not eroding, even as on-device AI grows.
evidence.source: Data Center Dynamics, citing Microsoft Q1 2025 earnings call

evidence.claim: Apple, despite having the resources to build its own frontier model, pays Google approximately $1B/year to license Gemini as the backbone of Apple Intelligence. This reveals that even the world's most valuable hardware company cannot cost-effectively replicate frontier model capability -- it outsources cognition to cloud AI providers and retains only the interface layer, confirming that frontier model ownership remains concentrated among a small number of cloud-scale operators (Google, OpenAI, Anthropic).
evidence.source: TechCrunch, reporting Apple-Google Gemini partnership, January 2026

evidence.claim: Agentic AI -- the fastest-growing enterprise AI deployment pattern, projected to reach a $9-10B market in 2026 with Gartner predicting 40% of enterprise apps will embed agents by end-of-2026 -- structurally requires frontier cloud models at the orchestration layer. The economics documented by practitioners are explicit: expensive frontier models handle complex reasoning and orchestration, mid-tier models handle standard tasks, and SLMs handle high-frequency execution. Cloud does not lose the agent stack; it owns the top of it where the value and margin sit.
evidence.source: Firecrawl / Agentic AI Trends 2026; Gartner forecast cited in tech-insider.org enterprise agentic AI analysis

power-grid-binding-constraint-ai.name: Power Grid as the Binding Constraint for AI
power-grid-binding-constraint-ai.whatItIs: Building and training advanced AI happens inside data centers, which are giant warehouses of computer chips that run nonstop and draw enormous amounts of electricity. For years the main limit on AI was getting enough chips. Now the bigger limit is getting enough electricity and a physical connection to the power grid, because the grid is the network of wires and substations that carries power from generators to buildings. In many places that grid is full or slow to expand, so the company with secured power, not the company with the most chips or money, is often the one that can actually grow.
power-grid-binding-constraint-ai.whatsHappening: As of mid-2026 the constraint has clearly shifted from chips to electricity. The World Economic Forum in May 2026 framed grid connectivity as a strategic bottleneck, noting AI workloads already consume tens of gigawatts and could approach hundreds of gigawatts by the end of the decade. The core mismatch: an AI data center can be planned and built in two to three years, but getting it connected to the grid can take far longer. DataCenterKnowledge reported in May 2026 that projects reaching operation in 2025 averaged roughly seven to eight years from queue to running, now split into about three years waiting for an interconnection agreement plus four more years of construction and grid upgrades after approval. A hidden choke point is hardware: lead times for the large transformers that connect facilities to the grid stretched from about 50 weeks in 2021 to 160-plus weeks in 2026. The squeeze is now visibly slowing growth. Fortune reported in March 2026 a "bend in the trajectory," with Q4 2025 capacity additions of just 25 gigawatts (down about 50 percent) and analysts estimating only about a third of the announced pipeline will actually get built. In response, hyperscalers are bypassing the grid by buying their own dedicated power: Meta signed nuclear deals for up to 6.6 gigawatts (Oklo, Vistra, TerraPower) and added a 20-year Constellation agreement in June 2025, while also turning to faster-to-build natural gas. Regulators are scrambling too: the US energy regulator FERC is due to finalize a national large-load interconnection framework (Docket RM26-4) by the end of June 2026, and in Texas, ERCOT is sorting through 226 gigawatts of large-load requests, about 73 percent of which are data centers.
power-grid-binding-constraint-ai.leans: Hyperscaler Self-Help (and the moat it creates)
power-grid-binding-constraint-ai.rationale: Skip is wrong: everyone agrees the constraint is real, but there's genuine disagreement on its severity and durability, which is a substantive question, not manufactured sides. Tradeoff is wrong: this isn't one axis with two poles, because the regulatory and efficiency camps make orthogonal claims about what *determines* the outcome rather than just sitting at the severe/mild ends. Multiple (4) fits after one collapse. I folded "Grid Constraint as Competitive Moat" into "Hyperscaler Self-Help": both agree the constraint is real, persistent enough to matter, and rewards whoever has secured power. Moat is just Self-Help seen from the investor's seat, not an independent answer to the severity/durability question, so presenting it separately would be padding. The remaining four are genuinely distinct: Fatal Bottleneck (decade-long sector-wide ceiling), Self-Help (filters losers, not leaders), Regulatory (outcome is politically contingent, two years vs. fifteen), and Efficiency (demand curves overstated). Evidence leans Self-Help: the dedicated-generation deals are observable, dated facts already happening, the Fatal Bottleneck data is exactly what motivates self-help rather than refuting it, the pure sector-wide-ceiling claim is undercut by leaders demonstrably routing around it, and the Efficiency camp is weakened by Jevons (efficiency historically expands demand). Not forcing balance: the survey's own notes converge on "multi-year friction favoring incumbents/self-help" over "decade-long structural ceiling," with the regulatory timeline as the legitimate open swing variable.

camps.label: Grid as Fatal Bottleneck
camps.proponents: NERC (North American Electric Reliability Corporation); Goldman Sachs Research (infrastructure/utilities analysts); RMI (Rocky Mountain Institute); Dominion Energy (in regulatory filings admitting capacity limits in Northern Virginia); PJM CEO David Mills; AEP CEO Bill Fehrman; IEA (International Energy Agency); RAND Corporation (national security framing)
camps.steelman:
The power grid is not a friction cost to be engineered around -- it is a multi-layered structural ceiling that operates on a fundamentally different clock than AI deployment, and the mismatch is compounding in ways that markets cannot self-correct in time to matter.

The core logic runs as follows. AI training and inference infrastructure can be planned and built in 18 to 36 months. Grid interconnection, by contrast, now takes a median of five years just to clear the queue, and that is before transmission upgrades are built. There are currently more than 2.2 terawatts of generation and storage projects stranded in US interconnection queues -- nearly double the country's entire installed capacity -- and the attrition rate is brutal: only 19% of projects that entered queues between 2000 and 2019 had reached commercial operation by end of 2024. The physical equipment layer is no better: transformer lead times have extended from 24 to 30 months pre-2020 to four to five years today, driven by a 274% surge in demand for generator step-up transformers since 2019. Electrical equipment is under 10% of total data center cost and 100% of the blocking variable.

The constraint is not evenly distributed -- it is geographically concentrated in exactly the places where AI infrastructure wants to be. Dominion Energy has publicly admitted it cannot guarantee power delivery to customers in Ashburn, Northern Virginia, the densest data center market on earth, due to transmission congestion. PJM, the largest grid operator in the world covering 65 million people, has been so overwhelmed that it paused new interconnection applications entirely in 2022 and, when it reopened, received 800 requests for 220 gigawatts of new power that it cannot process. PJM's own CEO has stated the situation is "not tenable" and that the region has "years, not decades" to fix it. AEP's CEO warned that without immediate action these conversations will be unchanged in ten years.

The reliability picture is not improving -- it is deteriorating faster than projections. NERC's January 2026 Long-Term Reliability Assessment projects summer peak demand will surge 224 GW over the next decade, 69% higher than NERC itself projected just one year earlier, with AI data centers identified as the primary driver. Thirteen of 23 North American assessment areas now face elevated or high resource adequacy risk. On May 4, 2026, NERC issued a rare Level 3 Essential Actions Alert -- its highest-urgency notification -- after multiple documented events where more than 1,000 MW of AI compute load dropped off the grid in seconds, threatening cascading frequency collapse. The bidirectional fragility is structural: the grid was not designed for loads this large, this concentrated, or this electrically sensitive.

The geopolitical dimension sharpens the argument. Connecting a new US facility to the grid takes 4 to 10 years while AI cycles run 18 to 36 months. This means the constraint does not just slow incumbents uniformly -- it reshapes who wins. Nations and actors with state-directed grid build-out, or with legacy overcapacity, gain a structural lead that cannot be purchased away at current market speeds. The US dependence on imported power equipment, including transformers sourced from China, adds a supply chain vulnerability on top of the domestic queue backlog. Goldman Sachs estimates $720 billion in grid investment is needed through 2030; the IEA projects US data center electricity demand rising 130% by 2030. The capital exists. The physical supply chain and regulatory pathway do not scale at the required rate.

The strongest version of this view holds that the bottleneck is not any single chokepoint but a system of interdependent constraints -- interconnection queues, transformer manufacturing capacity, transmission permitting, and grid stability requirements -- each operating on multi-year timescales with no short circuit available. Markets can route around any one of these; they cannot simultaneously compress all of them. The result is not a speed bump but a rate-limiting step on the entire AI compute buildout, with compounding effects on who can deploy at scale, where, and when.

evidence.claim: NERC's January 2026 Long-Term Reliability Assessment projects US summer peak demand will surge 224 GW over the next decade -- 69% higher than NERC's own 2024 projection -- with AI data centers as the primary driver. Thirteen of 23 North American assessment areas face elevated or high resource adequacy risk. On May 4, 2026, NERC issued a Level 3 Essential Actions Alert after multiple events where 1,000+ MW of AI compute load dropped off the grid in seconds, threatening cascading frequency instability.
evidence.source: NERC Long-Term Reliability Assessment (January 2026) and NERC Level 3 Alert (May 4, 2026), North American Electric Reliability Corporation

evidence.claim: More than 2.2 terawatts of generation and storage projects are stranded in US interconnection queues -- nearly double current installed grid capacity. Average interconnection time has grown from under two years in 2008 to nearly five years today. Only 19% of projects requesting interconnection between 2000 and 2019 had become operational by end of 2024. PJM CEO David Mills stated the situation is 'not tenable' and the region has 'years, not decades' to fix it.
evidence.source: RMI: 'The Interconnection Queue Continues to Be a Barrier to US Economic Competitiveness'; TechCrunch: 'The biggest US power grid is under strain from AI' (May 2026)

evidence.claim: Goldman Sachs Research projects data center power demand will surge 220% by 2030 from 2023 levels and estimates $720 billion in grid investment is needed through 2030. Despite $650bn+ in Big Tech AI spending planned for 2026, nearly half of US data center projects face delays or cancellation due to grid and component bottlenecks. Transformer lead times have extended to four to five years, up from 24 to 30 months pre-2020, driven by a 274% increase in generator step-up transformer demand since 2019.
evidence.source: Goldman Sachs Research (April 2026 update); PV Magazine USA: 'US transformer market faces severe supply constraints as lead times extend to four years' (May 2026)

camps.label: Hyperscaler Self-Help (and the Moat It Creates)
camps.proponents: Microsoft (Satya Nadella, public earnings disclosures), Google/Alphabet (Sundar Pichai, Kairos Power SMR deal), Meta (Mark Zuckerberg, 6.6 GW nuclear commitment), Amazon/AWS (Susquehanna nuclear acquisition), xAI (Elon Musk, Colossus deployment), SemiAnalysis (Dylan Patel, analyst), Value Add VC (infrastructure capex analysis), Power Magazine infrastructure analysts
camps.steelman:
The power grid constraint is real but it is not a symmetric problem. It is a selection mechanism that separates players who can self-provision energy infrastructure from those who cannot, and the hyperscalers are solving it at a scale that forecloses the option for everyone else.

The core argument has three interlocking parts.

First, capital buys exit from the grid. Microsoft, Google, Meta, and Amazon are collectively spending $660-690 billion in 2026 capex, roughly 75% of which targets AI infrastructure including power. They are signing 20-year nuclear PPAs, restarting decommissioned plants, deploying onsite gas turbines behind the meter, and contracting SMR fleets a decade out. This is not hope that the grid improves; it is a deliberate architectural decision to treat the public grid as irrelevant to their timeline. A hyperscaler that controls its own gigawatt of generation does not wait in a 7-year interconnection queue. It ships revenue.

Second, speed-to-power compounds into speed-to-revenue, which becomes a structural moat. An AI cloud deployment generates roughly $10-12 billion in annual revenue per gigawatt online. Getting a 400 MW cluster live six months earlier through onsite generation yields billions in incremental revenue and months of model training advantage. A behind-the-meter gas plant can be permitted and commissioned in 18 months versus a grid interconnect that takes four to seven years in constrained markets like Northern Virginia or ERCOT. xAI proved the extreme case: deploying over 500 MW of truck-mounted turbines near Colossus in Memphis weeks after site selection, entirely bypassing the grid. The legal and environmental controversy around that deployment is incidental to the strategic point: the option existed, was exercised, and yielded a running supercomputer. Only entities with the capital, legal teams, and infrastructure relationships to execute that kind of rapid deployment can access it.

Third, the moat self-reinforces because early grid capacity reservation is now permanently captured. Sites in Northern Virginia, the primary US hyperscale market, face approximately 7-year interconnection delays and have effectively stopped accepting new large-scale requests. Operators who secured grid capacity before 2024 hold assets that cannot be replicated on any near-term timeline, commanding premium valuations. Smaller AI companies, cloud challengers, and national AI programs in less wealthy countries face a structural constraint that money alone cannot quickly overcome because the equipment supply chains are also constrained: transformer lead times average 128 weeks (up from 52 pre-2020), turbine blade manufacturing is concentrated in four Western suppliers, and nuclear construction timelines are measured in decades not years.

The net effect is that the power grid constraint acts as a filter that concentrates AI compute capability into the five or six entities globally that can self-provision energy at gigawatt scale, write $10-20 billion nuclear contracts, and absorb multi-year capital commitment with negative free cash flow. For those entities, the grid constraint is temporary friction. For everyone else, it is close to a ceiling. The constraint does not slow AI progress overall; it routes AI progress through the hyperscalers and deepens the dependency of every other AI developer, enterprise, and nation-state on their platforms.

evidence.claim: Hyperscalers are collectively spending $660-690 billion in 2026 capex, with power infrastructure a core allocation. Microsoft disclosed $80 billion in unfulfilled Azure orders specifically because it cannot connect GPUs to power, and grid connections in major markets take 4+ years. This quantifies the scale of self-help investment required and why grid dependency is being abandoned.
evidence.source: Introl Blog / hyperscaler capex analysis, citing Satya Nadella earnings disclosures and market data

evidence.claim: An AI cloud deployment generates roughly $10-12 billion in annual revenue per gigawatt online. A behind-the-meter gas plant can be commissioned in 18 months versus a 4-7 year grid interconnect queue. xAI demonstrated the extreme version by deploying over 500 MW of onsite gas turbines at Colossus in Memphis, entirely bypassing grid interconnection. In Texas alone, barely more than 1 GW of data center load requests were approved in a full year despite tens of gigawatts submitted monthly.
evidence.source: SemiAnalysis, 'How AI Labs Are Solving the Power Crisis: The Onsite Gas Deep Dive' (Dylan Patel)

evidence.claim: Meta committed to 6.6 GW of nuclear agreements including a 1.2 GW campus with Oklo in Ohio and a 20-year PPA with Constellation. Google signed the first US corporate SMR fleet deal with Kairos Power for 500 MW. Microsoft signed a 20-year agreement to restart Three Mile Island for 835 MW. These are not hedges; they are decade-scale energy supply chains that no startup or mid-tier cloud provider can replicate.
evidence.source: Fierce Network / Trellis.net / Nuclear power for AI data centers roundup

camps.label: Regulatory Timeline as the Swing Variable
camps.proponents: Rocky Mountain Institute (RMI) energy analysts; International Energy Agency (IEA) under Executive Director Fatih Birol; Bipartisan Policy Center energy team; Senator Tom Cotton (DATA Act, 2026); DOE / FERC rulemaking proponents; energy-policy legal community (White & Case, Day Pitney); techno-statecraft analysts covering the federal power grab for AI infrastructure
camps.steelman: The power grid is not a fixed ceiling -- it is a policy-set variable. The physical electrons, wires, and generation capacity required to power the next decade of AI build-out already exist or are already in development: roughly 2.2 to 2.3 terawatts of generation and storage projects sit in U.S. interconnection queues right now, nearly double the entire installed generating capacity of the country. The bottleneck is not atoms or physics -- it is the administrative machinery that moves projects through permitting, interconnection studies, and tariff approvals. That machinery is slow by design (it was built for a stable-load era), not by necessity. The average time from interconnection request to commercial operation rose from under two years in 2008 to five to eight years by 2024-2025 -- a 3-4x degradation that tracks precisely with the accumulation of procedural layers, not with any increase in physical scarcity. This means the constraint is institutionally reversible. Jurisdictions that compress those timelines -- through FERC rulemaking, cluster-study reforms, first-ready/first-served queue management, permitting streamlining under NEPA, or outright off-grid exemptions like the proposed DATA Act -- can effectively unlock latent supply that is already partially financed and sited. The IEA has made this competitive dynamic explicit: countries that offer secure, rapid grid access will capture disproportionate shares of global AI investment. Dublin and Amsterdam already demonstrated the downside: permitting moratoriums caused hyperscalers to relocate compute buildouts to less-constrained jurisdictions. The implication is that the grid constraint is real but jurisdiction-specific and policy-elastic, not a universal physical ceiling. The winners in the AI infrastructure race will not be determined by who has the most power in the ground -- power is available everywhere -- but by who reforms their interconnection and permitting regimes fastest. That is a governance sprint, not an engineering problem, and governance sprints can be won in 12-36 months if political will is present, as the Trump EO (July 2025), the DOE directive to FERC (October 2025), the FERC PJM co-location order (December 2025), and the DATA Act (January 2026) all demonstrate is at least partially present. On this view, the binding constraint is not the grid itself but the regulatory clock -- and regulatory clocks can be reset.

evidence.claim: 2.2-2.3 terawatts of generation and storage projects are stuck in U.S. interconnection queues -- nearly double current installed capacity -- demonstrating that physical supply is not the bottleneck, procedural backlog is. Average interconnection wait times rose from under 2 years in 2008 to nearly 5-8 years by 2024-2025.
evidence.source: Rocky Mountain Institute, 'The Interconnection Queue Continues to Be a Barrier to US Economic Competitiveness' (2025); Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory Queued Up 2025 data cited therein

evidence.claim: IEA warns that 20% of planned global data center projects face delays due to grid constraints, and explicitly frames this as a regulatory competition: 'countries that provide secure, affordable and rapid access to electricity will be one step ahead,' with jurisdictions offering faster grid-connection timelines positioned to capture disproportionate AI investment growth.
evidence.source: IEA, 'Data centre electricity use surged in 2025, even with tightening bottlenecks driving a scramble for solutions' (IEA News, 2026); IEA, 'Key Questions on Energy and AI' executive summary

evidence.claim: The DATA Act (introduced January 2026) proposes to exempt fully off-grid data center power systems from FERC interconnection queue procedures entirely, targeting deployment timelines of 12-24 months versus the current 5+ year average -- illustrating that regulatory redesign, not new physical infrastructure, is the lever that compresses the constraint by 3-5x.
evidence.source: Introl Blog, 'DATA Act 2026: Off-Grid Power for AI Data Centers' (2026), covering Sen. Tom Cotton's Decentralized Access to Technology Alternatives Act

camps.label: Efficiency Will Soften the Squeeze
camps.proponents: Epoch AI (research org tracking AI compute trends); NVIDIA (hardware vendor, Blackwell efficiency claims); Google DeepMind / Google Infrastructure (demand response commitments and per-prompt efficiency benchmarks); Anthropic (curtailment and flexible load pledges); techno-optimist investors at a16z; academic researchers publishing on efficiency-dominant AI electricity scenarios (e.g., Coignard et al., arXiv 2603.10498)
camps.steelman:
The "power grid as hard ceiling" thesis conflates physical capacity with effective throughput, and mistakes a temporary supply-demand lag for a structural law. The steelman runs as follows.

Three compounding curves are each independently powerful, and together they dwarf the raw megawatt forecasts that alarm pessimists.

First, hardware efficiency per token is improving at a rate that makes generation-over-generation comparisons embarrassing. NVIDIA Blackwell GB200 NVL72 delivers more than 10x throughput per megawatt versus Hopper; Blackwell Ultra reaches 50x versus Hopper. A 1-MW facility that served 180,000 tokens per second on Hopper hardware serves 1.8-5 million tokens per second on equivalent power with Blackwell. Every new chip generation that deploys into the existing power envelope multiplies the effective AI capacity the grid already delivers without adding a single new transmission line.

Second, algorithmic and software efficiency is compounding on top of hardware gains. Epoch AI's empirical analysis of frontier models finds pre-training compute efficiency improving at roughly 3x per year (doubling every 7.6 months), and inference costs halving every two months when controlling for output quality. Google reported a 33x reduction in energy per median Gemini prompt between May 2024 and May 2025 through software optimization alone. DeepSeek demonstrated that distillation and architectural choices (Mixture of Experts activating 5-10% of parameters per token) can achieve near-frontier capability at a small fraction of the compute. The implication: the AI capability that requires X megawatts today requires X/3 megawatts in a year and X/9 in two years, assuming constant demand volume. Grid capacity is a fixed numerator; efficiency improvements compound in the denominator.

Third, demand-side flexibility means that even the raw megawatt load AI does consume need not hit the grid as a hard synchronous spike. AI training runs are batch workloads -- schedulable, deferrable, and inherently interruptible. In Phoenix in May 2025, a data center cut load by 25% for three hours during peak demand with no hardware changes and no service interruptions. Google has signed demand response agreements with Indiana Michigan Power and Tennessee Valley Authority targeting ML workloads specifically. Anthropic is deploying curtailment systems at its owned facilities. The Electric Power Research Institute's DCFlex coalition estimates flexible AI load could unlock more than 100 GW of demand response capacity in the existing U.S. power system. This makes AI data centers more like a pressure-release valve than a hard constraint -- they absorb surplus power and yield it back during stress periods.

The deepest version of this argument is not that demand won't grow, but that the grid constraint is a temporal friction, not a structural ceiling. The lag between interconnection applications and energized substations is 3-5 years; AI hardware generations turn over every 18-24 months. By the time the grid buildout the pessimists say is impossible arrives, the hardware running on it will be 10-50x more efficient per token. The AI progress that matters -- capability per dollar, per watt, per second -- continues to compound in the numerator even while the watt denominator grows more slowly. A slower grid does not freeze capability; it reprices it and routes around it through efficiency.

evidence.claim: NVIDIA Blackwell GB200 NVL72 delivers more than 10x tokens per megawatt versus Hopper; Blackwell Ultra (GB300 NVL72) reaches up to 50x throughput per megawatt and 35x lower cost per token versus Hopper. A 1-MW Hopper facility generating 180,000 tokens/sec becomes a 1.8M+ tokens/sec facility at the same power draw on Blackwell.
evidence.source: NVIDIA Perspectives blog: 'NVIDIA Blackwell: 10x More Tokens Per Watt'

evidence.claim: Epoch AI finds pre-training compute efficiency improving at roughly 3x per year (doubling every 7.6 months). Inference compute efficiency drops 3x per year holding quality fixed. Google independently reported a 33x reduction in energy per median Gemini prompt between May 2024 and May 2025 through software optimization alone.
evidence.source: Epoch AI: 'The least understood driver of AI progress'; corroborated by Epoch AI Trends dashboard (epoch.ai/trends) and cited in IEA Energy and AI report summary

evidence.claim: Flexible AI data center load -- specifically ML training workloads -- can defer, throttle, or shift power consumption during grid stress events. A Phoenix data center cut load 25% for three hours in May 2025 with no service interruption. The Electric Power Research Institute estimates flexible AI load could unlock over 100 GW of demand response in the existing U.S. power system.
evidence.source: CPower Energy / GridFuture coverage; Google Latitude Media report on demand response ML agreements; Anthropic 'Build AI in America' (Feb 2026)

long-context-windows-1m-10m-tokens.name: Long-Context Windows Hit 1M to 10M Tokens
long-context-windows-1m-10m-tokens.whatItIs: A context window is the amount of text and other data an AI model can hold in mind at once when it answers you, measured in tokens (a token is roughly three quarters of a word). For years most models could only read a couple hundred thousand tokens per request, so you had to feed them small slices of a document. Now the leading models can take in one million tokens or more in a single request, which is enough to read an entire codebase, a long legal contract, or a whole book at once and reason across all of it.
long-context-windows-1m-10m-tokens.whatsHappening: As of mid-2026, a one million token window has become the default for frontier models rather than a premium add-on. The turning point was March 13, 2026, when Anthropic made the 1M window generally available for Claude Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6 and dropped the old long-prompt surcharge, so a 900,000 token request now costs the same per token as a tiny one. Anthropic's newer models continued the trend: Opus 4.8 (May 28, 2026) and Claude Fable 5 (June 9, 2026) both ship 1M. Google's Gemini 3 Pro and Gemini 3.1 Pro, plus OpenAI's GPT-5.5 (1M in the API, though 400K in Codex), also sit at 1M, and by June 2026 at least thirteen hosted models offer 1M or more. The headline 10 million token figure belongs to Meta's open-weight Llama 4 Scout, but it is widely viewed as a marketing number: independent reviewers note it is not competitive on reasoning at those lengths. The big practical story is the squeeze on retrieval systems (RAG): for bounded, one-off tasks you can now just paste everything in, though for high-volume querying RAG stays far cheaper, so teams are settling on hybrids. The recurring caveat from researchers is context rot: most 1M models lose accuracy past roughly 500,000 tokens, and facts buried in the middle get missed.
long-context-windows-1m-10m-tokens.rationale:
The five proposed camps collapse hard. Context-Rot Skeptics, Open-Weight Realists, and Hybrid Architects are not rival worldviews about whether long context displaces RAG; they are the shared empirical and economic premises that everyone, including the abolitionists, already accepts. "Recall degrades past ~500K," "10M open-weight needs hardware you don't have," and "use cheap retrieval for frequent narrow queries, long context for one-off deep reasoning" are observations no serious practitioner disputes. They are evidence, not stances. Treating them as separate camps manufactures a five-way debate out of one real axis.

That real axis is a genuine tradeoff with two poles, and it is the survey's own stated primary fault line: for a given task, do you put everything in the prompt or do you retrieve? This is not "camps" of people; it is a per-task engineering decision the same team makes differently on Monday and Tuesday. Even the loudest "abolitionists" (Willison's actual position is "long context makes RAG optional for bounded tasks," not "RAG is dead") concede scale economics; even RAG pragmatists concede the escape-hatch use. The disagreement is purely about where the crossover point sits, which is the signature of a tradeoff, not a binary clash of incompatible beliefs. So I collapse to tradeoff rather than multiple/binary.

Leans toward retrieval-as-backbone because the contested claim is economic and the economics are not close at production scale: re-sending 100K-1M tokens per request versus a vector lookup is two-to-three orders of magnitude difference in cost and latency, and prompt caching narrows but does not erase it. Combined with the well-replicated lost-in-the-middle / context-rot degradation in the exact 500K-1M range the abolitionist case depends on, the weight of evidence favors retrieval staying essential, with long context as a powerful complement for bounded, low-frequency, deep-reasoning tasks. Not 50/50, but the abolitionist pole holds real ground for those bounded cases, so it stays a live tradeoff rather than a settled question (skip).

camps.label: Stuff the Context Window
camps.proponents: Google DeepMind (Gemini 1.5 technical report, Feb 2024); Google Cloud platform documentation; researchers in the "Long Context vs. RAG for LLMs" evaluation (arXiv 2501.01880); practitioners who adopted Gemini 2M-context pipelines in place of RAG infrastructure (documented in Google Cloud engineering blogs)
camps.steelman:
Million-token context windows represent a genuine architectural displacement of RAG for a large and growing class of tasks, not merely a complementary upgrade. The core argument runs as follows.

RAG is a workaround for a constraint that no longer exists. Chunking, embedding, vector indexing, and similarity search exist entirely because models could not hold large corpora in memory at once. Every step in that pipeline introduces compounding error: chunking severs the structural relationships between functions and their call sites, between clauses and their referents, between data and its schema; similarity search retrieves textually proximate passages rather than causally or logically relevant ones; ranked retrieval makes a hard bet on which k fragments matter before the model has reasoned at all. When the entire corpus fits in the context window, every one of these failure modes vanishes. The model sees all dependencies simultaneously, can reason across distant sections without a retrieval intermediary, and never loses information to a bad chunking boundary or a misleading embedding.

The empirical record now supports this at scale. Native long-context processing of a full document outperforms k-NN retrieval over the same document in head-to-head QA: on Les Miserables (710K tokens), full-book context yields 91.4% AutoAIS versus 84.8% for a 4K-token RAG slice of the same content. Retrieval accuracy at 1M tokens exceeds 99.7% on text and reaches 100% on video and audio modalities, where RAG-based systems have no meaningful multimodal baseline at all. For knowledge bases that fit within 2M tokens, practitioners can skip the retrieval layer entirely, reduce architectural complexity, and combine context caching to bring per-query costs down 60-70% on repeated access patterns.

The architectural simplification is not cosmetic. RAG pipelines require tuning at every joint: chunk size, overlap, embedding model, index type, retrieval strategy, re-ranker, and prompt assembly. Each joint is a point of silent failure and a maintenance burden. A single long-context call collapses all of that into one prompt, one model call, and one point of failure. For bounded, moderately stable corpora such as a company's legal agreements, a product's full documentation, a software repository under one million lines, or a regulatory filing set, the complexity-vs-accuracy trade-off decisively favors stuffing the context.

In-context learning further extends the displacement. Gemini 1.5 Pro, given a 500-page grammar manual for Kalamang (a language with fewer than 200 speakers and virtually no web presence), achieved near-human translation quality without any fine-tuning or retrieval index, demonstrating that long-context models can absorb and apply entirely new knowledge domains from documents alone. This capability has no RAG equivalent: RAG retrieves from an existing index; it cannot teach the model new structured reasoning patterns from scratch.

The strongest version of this camp's claim is not that RAG is dead for all use cases, but that RAG's historical necessity is gone for bounded corpora, that long-context native processing is now the correct default starting point, and that the burden of proof has reversed: you should reach for RAG only after establishing that your corpus exceeds practical context limits or that latency and token-cost constraints at your query volume make repeated full-context calls untenable.

evidence.claim: Gemini 1.5 Pro achieves 99.7% needle-in-haystack recall at 1M tokens and 100% accuracy on video and audio retrieval across 10.5 hours of video and 107 hours of audio, and the paper explicitly states the model beats RAG-based systems for all modalities. On Les Miserables QA, full-book context (710K tokens) scored 91.4% AutoAIS versus 84.8% for 4K retrieved tokens, a direct head-to-head showing native long-context outperforms retrieval.
evidence.source: Gemini 1.5: Unlocking multimodal understanding across millions of tokens of context (Google DeepMind, arXiv 2403.05530, February 2024)

evidence.claim: Long-context LLMs consistently outperform RAG in question-answering benchmarks, especially for Wikipedia-based questions. The paper also finds that summarization-based retrieval is competitive with full long-context but that chunk-based RAG (the dominant production form) lags behind, and emphasizes that context relevance is systematically underweighted in prior RAG evaluations.
evidence.source: Long Context vs. RAG for LLMs: An Evaluation and Revisits (arXiv 2501.01880, January 2025)

evidence.claim: Google's own platform documentation states that the default approach is now to put all tokens directly into the context window, and that Gemini models were purpose-built for in-context learning in ways that make long-context a simpler and often superior alternative to RAG infrastructure. Context caching reduces repeated long-context query costs by 60-70%, making the economics competitive.
evidence.source: Google Cloud Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform — Long Context documentation

camps.label: Keep retrieval as the backbone
camps.proponents: Database and search infrastructure vendors (Redis, Pinecone, Weaviate, Elasticsearch); vector-database researchers and practitioners; enterprise AI architects at firms with large, dynamic, or compliance-sensitive corpora; ML engineers who have benchmarked long-context models in production; Chroma Research (context-rot empirical study, 2025); academic authors of "Lost in the Middle" (Liu et al., 2024) and "Context Length Alone Hurts LLM Performance Despite Perfect Retrieval" (arXiv 2510.05381, 2025)
camps.steelman:
Million-token context windows are a genuine capability advance, but they solve a narrow slice of the retrieval problem while leaving the hardest parts untouched. The case for keeping retrieval as the backbone rests on four interlocking arguments.

First, raw context size does not equal usable context. Empirical research on 18 frontier models including GPT-4.1, Claude 4, and Gemini 2.5 shows that every tested model degrades measurably as input length grows, and the degradation begins well before the advertised maximum. Chroma Research's 2025 "context rot" study found systematic accuracy decline on simple retrieval and text-replication tasks at scales far below each model's headline window. A separate 2025 paper (arXiv 2510.05381) demonstrated that performance drops 13.9 to 85 percent even when all irrelevant tokens are replaced with whitespace or masked entirely, meaning the penalty is not about distraction but about input length itself. The foundational "Lost in the Middle" finding (Liu et al.) showed a U-shaped attention pattern where models reliably attend to the beginning and end of a prompt but lose roughly 40 percent of facts placed in the middle. Newer models have narrowed this gap on synthetic needle-in-a-haystack benchmarks but have not closed it on realistic multi-hop reasoning tasks.

Second, the economics of stuffing large contexts into every query are prohibitive at production scale.
